title: 2025/07/04 - TI (Part 2)
displayTitle: 2025-07-04 - Transport and Infrastructure Committee - Building and Construction (Small Stand-alone Dwellings) Amendment Bill [Part 2]
topic: Building and Construction (Small Stand-alone Dwellings) Amendment Bill
description: Friday, 4 July 2025 | Transport and Infrastructure Committee Building and Construction (Small Stand-alone Dwellings) Amendment Bill Hearing of Evidence - John Verry - Simon Litten - Sky Sigal - Bruce Russell Waimakariri District Council - Mayor Dan Gordon - Craig Atkinson - Chrissy and Steve Watson - Matt Rutledge - Ross Wakefield Co-Lab - David Samson
descriptionSummary: Building and Construction (Small Stand-alone Dwellings) Amendment Bill. John Verry. Simon Litten

Okay. Kira Koto, thank you for the opportunity. Um, I'm submitting in support of the bill, and I think that it's good for increasing housing options and affordability for people, especially in the outside. The urban areas would just make a note, which I'm sure the committee is all aware of, that if there are certain things to be deregulated, it's important that there's not a loss of protection for the homeowners, that there's not a detriment to public safety and there's environmental responsibility,

and I suppose to be constructive and probably being mentioned before that the work should be performed by licensed building practitioners, registered architects, et cetera. And a contentious issue probably, that there should be a national insurance scheme, so that rather than any failure or defects, the remediation of the same, falling to the professionals concerned, they should have sufficient long lasting insurance.

Because I think it's unfair, like the changes to the building act a few years ago, that the councils and the rate payers bear the cost as the last person standing. So I think that's an important thing, which the committee is no doubt well aware of and will turn their expertise to.

Um, and I think the protections in the building act like the implied warranties, the certifications that these are safeguards that need to be retained. And there also I think, needs to be clarity for the average person when these changes are brought about and implemented so that they're aware and they fully understand, because many will be like myself with not much of a technical expertise, but a concern in the fact that when we do something, it's lawful and it's safe and it lasts.

Then there's the environmental issues, I suppose, like making sure there's adequate disposal of the wastewater and the infrastructure can cope. And in a rural setting, this is probably more relevant to septic tanks than actually, um, infrastructure wastewater and sewage.

And maybe my final point, so you don't probably need to give me the notice, the, um, guidance or model bylaws so that when councils are setting district plans, that they've got some sort of national guidelines to go with. And I think this reduces the duplication so that rather than each district authority figuring out their own, if, if there's clear guidelines or even a model that they could adopt, that again, saves rate payers having to be at the brunt of additional costs, especially, um, in the selwyn district where the rates are going up horrendously.

So thank you for listening. That's my submission, and I'm happy to take any questions. John. Thank you. We've got almost two minutes left. Uh, Scott, first question. Thank you, John. Um, so I guess one of your points is that you, you suggested we need a national insurance scheme for, um, local building, uh, licensed building practitioners.

Do you see this as being government funded so that, because, um, we've heard about the, the challenge, potential challenge of getting insurance for, for these, these structures. So do you think this should be government funded? That's the first question. And the second one, I guess is the, um, the, the concern that some have expressed about territorial authorities being seen as the, the authority of, of to who will be ultimately responsible if a, if a building practitioner is no longer there to, to answer for problems for challenges down the line.

And yet they haven't been able to do a, a, a close look at things. So risks there. Are you suggesting that they need to issue a consent of some kind? Um, to start with the second point first, no, but I do think that there, there should be an insurance scheme and know it shouldn't be funded by the government.

It should be funded by the, um, licensed building practitioners, the architects, the engineers involved somehow rather. And then at the end of the day, it's obviously the homeowner who's getting the minor dwelling that's going to pay that, but I think that's fair enough because they get a benefit.

So they should be the, um, payers of the protection. And yes, it just seems unfair when rate payers get landed as the last resort because with the leaky homes and the number of architects, builders, et cetera, who went into liquidation and abdicated any responsibility and then left it to the poor rate, payers are doing their best in these hard economic times.

So I really do think that's a key issue that should be resolved or some clear guidance given Scott, John, thank you. We've, we've come to time. Um, but um, just having had 30 years of, uh, experience in local government, um, I could echo what you just said about rate, pay liability, horrible, horrible thing.

Right. Okay. Cool. Thank you very much. Uh, we've got now, Simon. Thank you. Welcome. Thank you, John. Really appreciate it. So Simon, we've got you down for five minutes. I'll be brief. I'm here to call attention again to the same issues we've already raised in detail through two prior submissions. The problem is not technical, it is avoidance and timidity at the national level. The proposal as it stands, signals reform, but cannot deliver. It promises relief, but breaks that promise through omissions and a failure to override local obstruction.

As the proposal stands in urban zones, three dwellings are permitted on lots of an eighth of an acre, but in rural families will be limited to one MRU on lots of 300 acres. That is not planning logic. It is discrimination to the benefit of unaffordable developers and poorly managed councils encoded as national policy.

MRU are small, single story, low impact and necessary. They pose no threat to rural amenity. Don't let local overlays intend to constrain expansion, often disguised as ecological or amenity claims. Rob real citizens of the chance to remain on their land and build intergenerational support.

Mru are not speculative developers. Avoid them. They're built only by families in need. When someone falls ill returns home requires care or assistance. Yet this proposal leaves them trapped caught between the commercial clutches of councils and developers of apartments they can't afford, don't want, and they don't come with fanau and without care.

If local councils can continue to block a family from digging a pad, creating outdoor space, because the MRU is small, forming a track to get to it or installing a septic tank because it, they're expecting a primary health first, and they should have this by right engineered if required by the zone, then they can block the MRU itself and all change with it.

It's that simple. We don't just recommend change, we implore you take the singular opportunity to do what only national law can do, override, obstruction, enable by right families, make this reform real Sky. Thank you very much. Um, questions?

Look, can I, can I ask you one? Yeah. And it's, um, from a planning point of view Yep. And of course this is, this is not about planning, this is about building consenting, but from planning point of view, when does rural cease to be rural? In other words, you know, how many properties could you or how many MOUs could you put on a, a property before you say, well, it's actually, it kind of no longer looks rural fair enough, no longer feels rural.

I believe that's enough of a distinction that we don't need to have the visibility for the whole peninsula of were removed from all houses and dwellings of people. These are very little buildings. Firstly, I support the Bill's intent and increasing housing supply and afford and affordability. Nothing that deregulation must come with adequate safeguards for the public. Two issues. Loss of protection without building consent. Without a building consent.

Home owners may lose legal practitioners and oversight, risk of non-compliant or substandard bills limited, uh, recourse for owners if things go wrong. I asked that safeguards be embedded in the legislation. Three safeguards recommended mandatory use of licensed building practitioners in LPCs who must carry compulsory insurance.

Written building contracts and disclosure of risk. Implied warranties must still apply even if no consent post-construction certificate of work or compliance statement. Four, reducing cost and increasing access to affordable housing. Removing the consenting process costs and delays is positive for one.

First home buyers downsizing retirees re relocatable transitional housing. I strongly support removing red tape for dwellings under a set size example, 70 square meters. Five. Clarification building costs still applies. Exception is for consent, not compliance.

eSafety elements must remain enforceable. Structure for fire safety with a tightness. Earthquake resilience. I support a public register of pre-approved designs for easier compliance. Fix. Environmental and pending considerations. Ensure consistencies with Research Management Act and local plans.

As council needs tools to manage one wastewater and stormwater are amenity impacts. Four, I mean, uh, uh, seven. Conclusion. I support the bill and with appropriate safeguards, balance, flexibility with legal and safety. Two minutes to go responsibility.

What I'm saying there, the consent still must, um, you know, while I'm quite happy that if the person considering the consent is okay, as long as it meets the criteria, because what you're saying is in your format was the fact that it must be built of light materials, et cetera, and still conform.

Thank you, Mr. Chair. And can I also acknowledge, uh, other members of the city committee? I, I'm Dan Gordon, the mayor of We, and with, uh, me as Aaron Hayes, the Council's Building, building Unit, um, manager. Uh, just a few points about, just to say as an introductory remarks that our Y Macary district is the fourth largest territorial authority in the south island.

We are located north of Christchurch in sheer boundaries with Christchurch City, uh, as well as Selwyn and Nui districts. And about a year ago, we, um, enjoyed the, uh, hosting of you, Mr. Chair, uh, in our district. So, and you're always welcome as there's any member of parliament to come and see what we're doing here.

Um, uh, our district ranges from urban townships such as rung y and to, to the remote high country farms, uh, of the Lees Valley. 60% of our residents live in the district's four largest towns, and another 6,000 live on rural residential lifestyle blocks. Y ery has been the growth district for decades, and there is a lot of interest for development here.

Between 1996 and 2023, our population has doubled and, and this high growth trend is expected to continue, uh, for the next, uh, 20 years. Indeed, I've been elected member for most of that time when I was first elected. We are at 38,000. Our district now sits at 74,000 in terms of population since making our submission on the bill, the council has recently, as in last week, finalized our proposed district plan that will enable between 14,000 and 17,000 dwellings to be built, uh, over the coming 30 years.

And we've included in that as a Tier one council, medium, uh, MDRS requirements as, uh, we were required to, uh, by law. Um, that's a bit of momentous decision for us, uh, in making, uh, that, and we will be publicly notified in the next, uh, week or so.

And then hopefully with few, with as few, uh, appeals as possible becomes operative. Uh, I, I I say with, um, base breath, um, you like our council position. Uh, the council supports the overall purpose of this bill, uh, particularly the intention to make it easier to build small standalone dwellings under conditions, including that councils must be notified prior to and on completion of the building work.

We specifically support the following aspects, the intent of the bill that will lower housing costs for small standalone dwellings. We think that's a really great outcome. Increasing the maximum size of the initial 60 square meters in the discussion document to 70 square meters, we also think is a really good outcome.

Excuse me. Um, the use of the project information, uh, memorandum or PIM that would notify the council, but involve less process time and costs in the building consent. We had suggested the use of PIMS in our first written submission on this matter. So we're really pleased to see that that's been incorporated.

While we support the intent of the bill, we just wish to highlight the significant work that we have already undertaken, uh, over the last, over several years to accommodate our growing community. And particularly we support a larger maximum floor area than what is proposed in the bill. Our recently finalized district plan had increased the maximum from 75 meters squared to 80 meters squared, or 90 meters squared, as outlined in 0.1, 0.3 of our written submission.

In this latest version of the district plan, we've also removed the requirement for the secondary dwelling to be located within 30 meters of the main dwelling, noting that we have approximately 6,000 rural residential or rural lifestyle blocks in the district that would benefit from greater flexibility.

We've also removed the one kitchen per dwelling, per dwelling rule that will better support extended family living arrangements. And I personal experience with this, with, uh, family that resided in our own home, uh, as we were growing up and we couldn't have more than one kitchen under the roof. It's been a change that I've personally pushed because I, I think the council should get, and government should get out of under people's roofs except for, um, uh, making sure that they, uh, meet the building code, but the rest is up to them in how they live.

Uh, in my personal view, uh, when preparing the written submission, we made an assumption that the Ministry for Business Innovation and Employment will provide guidance to support the legislative changes, similar to helpful guidance previously prepared by the ministry to support changes, uh, in the building sector.

We highlight the importance of this guidance and suggest that this be released as close to when the bill is passed, as is practical to allow councils in the building sector to prepare for this change in existing requirements. We just wanna make sure it's as smooth as possible and for everyone to be on the same page.

We do also want to highlight the use of the pim, uh, requires further consideration to ensure the PIM process is manageable, robust, and beneficial, uh, to all parties, particularly to clause eight. Reducing the timescale for council to issue a PIM from 20 to 10 days.

Owners will, uh, likely have a shorter timeframe through the PIM process than the current building consent process. And PIM applications will not require the same level of detail as a building consent application as a council, uh, that's issuing this and works in this space, just say 10 days is not realistic.

Dan, Dan, we've got four minutes left. Yep. Thank you. Um, I'm coming to my concluding points now anyway, um, in conclusion, we are looking, looking forward to these legislative changes, potentially having higher uptake of smaller dwellings, offering a greater level of housing choice in Yma and across the country.

We thank the committee for their time and the opportunity to speak today, and we're happy to take any questions. And so clearly I'll hand them over to Aaron because he's more expert than me. Very good, Thank you very much. Well, we've got two, uh, two hands up. So Cam and then Tangi. Uh, thank you chair. Uh, thank you Mr. Mayor as well. Um, look, uh, this has been a great submission you've put in.

I enjoyed reading it. The part that I've questioned about is how does the 10 days, uh, impact the, the 10 days period impact? Why? I would've thought like the things that are needed in a PIM are generally held on council, um, databases in a lot of ways in this modern age of information technology.

It seems a surprise that considering we've got computers that do these things, um, amalgamating it could take 20 days. So could you tell me why you think 10 is too small, too short? Aaron, who's, who's practically, uh, dealing with this? Okay, thank you. Um, yeah. So, uh, there's a difference between a limb, which is us, um, retrieving information and council system and providing it to someone about a property and a PIM where somebody provides a proposal to us for a building project that they have, and we have to assess that against certain rules.

So this bill proposes that council makes a statement about one, whether the, um, proposal actually meets the exemption criteria, and also, uh, whether it, uh, is subject to any natural hazards. And thirdly, um, a about whether any of our bylaws, uh, are met or compromised by the proposal.

Now, those are three parts of a process that we don't currently do with a standard Tim. So we are being asked to do more in less time, and we think that, uh, 20 days would be more reasonable for us to undertake that work. We're just being realistic about what we, yeah, we think we can deliver. The, the, um, the qualifying thing that would go for with that is that if we are forced to do a PIM in 10 days, we would have to have a pretty, um, tough acceptance criteria and would be kicking back a lot of applications at the first post, um,

if they didn't meet certain criteria. Another, um, part of that is that, you know, the, the documentation that people provide to us during a consent process, they provide that because they know that council's reviewing it and, and, uh, what they won't get a building consent if they don't provide the right level of documentation. The level of documentation they have to provide for a PIM is not clearly defined in this legislation.

So it's quite likely that we may get very light documentation and have to ask a lot of questions when the PI's being processed as well. Thank you. We've got a lot to a lot of natural hazards here, which we've had, and certainly post earthquakes, we've had a huge amount of experience at dealing with this. So we're just being realistic back to you in our suggestion.

It would make, we might as, let's make the process simple. We agree with that, but just give us the right length of time so we can do our job properly. Thank you, Tanya. Thank you, Mr. Chair. Thank you, Mr. Mayor. Um, my question was very similar to cams, actually, um, around the timeframe. And I was wondering, you know, your response there is that it might be that more information is required in the process of council, basically, uh, working through the, the PIM being issued.

H how would you feel about a, a 10 day work, a 10 working day, um, limit, but the ability to extend that, uh, as exists currently with other aspects of council if the information is not forthcoming? Or are you saying that that would effectively create some false expectations? Uh, it's possible that, uh, an ability could be provided to put the application on hold and the timeframe is suspended.

If we had to ask for further information. But, um, effectively then that stretching out the timeframe for somebody to receive a PIM anyway would, and it's inefficient. It would be far better for us to receive high quality applications and that it's very clear for property owners and their, their consultants that are using to provide the right information upfront and we can, you know, process an issue with PIM efficiently. Okay.

Ideally, and thank you for visiting yourself recently. Um, um, uh, Tony, um, we we're a council that has experienced at this. If we turn them around quicker, we will do so, but we are just asking for it to be realistic. We, we think this is great, what's being proposed, we just want it to practically work, and that's our experience and being a fast growing counselor, we're used to keeping these things moving, uh, with the level of growth that we're experiencing.

Uh, and we've gone away from that, that we just get academics sort of, uh, dribble that refers to design elements, and then builders of all levels have gotta be able to decipher that. Uh, now anybody could go out and design anything else, but if you just put three plans out, just basic plans of what the things are, and allow it for any iwi, then to pick it up and decide for how many they want to build or anybody else, it's not brain surgery. It reduces all the risks.

You can go through all the councils, you can deal with all the b******t and just look at what the details are that people can actually build small wellings that are affordable and, and sustainable. Uh, and that's what we've, uh, since, uh, Michael Joseph Savage, uh, and, and social housing, we've gone away from, from, uh, what, what housing can be in into million dollar mansions.

And, uh, businesses then look at themselves to, uh, focus in different areas, Iwis, that are looking at sort of housing their own people, uh, uh, a port that basically they've gotta go through the big building companies that they're playing way over $4,000 a square meter, uh, for complex, uh, buildings, uh, that we could be simplifying that, that they could be training their own people, that these plans could be like they built at certain schools.

Currently across the group building consents are consistently issued within 20 working, um, the two 20 working day statutory timeframe, um, provided adequate information provided this period's often significantly less for simple projects such as small standalone dwellings.

The amendment proposes to remove the requirement for the building consent for small standalone dwellings under the addition of Schedule one A of the building act with the addition of section 42 B three C prescribing that no building work on these buildings may begin before a PIM is issued under section 34, uh, by the TA As well as a number of others.

The amendment also introduces changes to section 33 and 34 of the Building Act. In the addition of section 35 A all in relation to project information memorandums or PIMS changes requiring the owner to submit with the PIM application. A description of building work along with design plans requiring TAs to issue a PIM within 10 working days in relation to small standalone dwellings, requesting additional information be supplied by TAs, which provides advice on whether the proposed building work is likely unlikely, or whether it's unclear as to whether the characteristics

of clause one of Schedule one A are complied with and requesting that we're applicable and assessment and information be provided by the TA in relation to natural hazards under Section 71 of the Building Act. The risk with how these amended sections have been drafted is that TAs may apply these sections with significant inconsistency.

Um, currently PIM applications are reviewed and issued by a member of the TA with no regulatory requirement for technical competency or qualification. Section 35 A in particular alludes to there being some responsibility on the TA to provide advice on critical design features such as overarching compliance with the building code, finished floor levels, protection of other property from fire, stormwater, et cetera, and in excess in an assessment of Section 71 in relation to natural hazards, these decisions would otherwise be made by a member

of the building Consent authority, who is required to hold an appropriate competency under BCA Regulation 10, um, required to regularly undertake training under BCA. Regulation 11 holds an appropriate qualification under BCA regulation 18 and have their decisions monitored through annual audit processes under BCA Regulation 17. With the introduction of Section 3 92 A removing the liability of councils and providing this information with the pim, the risk is that TAs will adopt a varying view on their responsibilities

with no overarching regulatory oversight, such as an accreditation requirement to help monitor this in. In addition, the risk that homeowners will rely on this information being provided at the discretion of councils with making, when making significant financial decisions, um, could lead to unnecessary conflict between councils and their communities. Currently, our inspection failure rates for building work under building consents range between 35 and 45% across the group.

This is a mixture of building work not being completed in accordance with the consented plans and required documents, either not being provided or being provided incomplete or inaccurate. This while knowing from the date the consent was issued that they are required and knowing that no CCC will be issued until council is satisfied that building work is completed in accordance with the building consent.

The proposed amendment places the responsibility on their owner to provide documents to councils within 20 working days of completion. This introduces the risk that the work may never be considered completed or not considered, completed for some time, placing the onus on councils to follow up on issued PIMS for these projects and potentially take enforcement action.

It also reduces the leverage available to councils to support homeowners to obtain accurate and complete documentation on completion without taking enforcement action. The proposed amendment places significant responsibility on building practitioners to complete the project in accordance with the legislative standards. Under the current scheme, notwithstanding the proposed improvements to disciplinary processes and publishing of the LBPs details, whose licenses have been suspended, we do not believe the current LBP scheme is robust enough to give confidence that homeowners are not gonna be negatively affected by the removal of the requirement to obtain a building consent, particularly in terms
